author: @Rebecka0001
text:
Tiffany, just wanted to say that we’re all here supporting you no matter what. I can only imagine how difficult it’s been navigating the grief of losing your mom. Your family is filled with so much love, and it really shows.

One thing I admire is that even though you loved living in Maui and wanted to stay there, you chose to do what was best for your kids. That says so much about the kind of mother you are and how much you care about their happiness, opinions, and well-being.

At the same time, please remember to take care of yourself too. You don’t always have to be strong or show the best version of yourself. We’re all human, and everyone goes through hard seasons in life. It’s okay to have difficult moments.

I truly love this family, and I hope you realize how much of a positive impact you have on so many of us who watch your videos. Thank you for choosing to share your life with us on YouTube. Sending you all so much love. ❤️

author: @cyndy7826
text: Thank you for loving HER !! It makes be so happy to see a younger woman cherish what true cooking was all about, from previous generations, and to have the desire to make the recipes ! Those were the days of whole foods, often direct from the farm, to local grocery stores. Women actually cooked all the meals every day and did not worry about added preservatives, hormones, or chemicals etc. We even had our own vegetable gardens. We were healthier: mentally, emotionally, and physically. I am a healthy 71 year old and had that very cookbook handed down to me from my mother; I learned how to cook, garden, and sew from my grandmother, mother, and aunts. While taking college prep classes, I took the local Betty Crocker Award contest, on a whim, and won; I had never taken a home economics class in school. I became a college educated professional and worked 44 years, while raising a family, but still used my Betty Crocker life skills, with gratitude, for the women who taught me the joy and fulfillment of gathering family and friends around a table to share, not only a meal, but the beauty and peace of human connection. I always included my three sons in meal preparation. I am teaching my granddaughter, including my grandsons, my Betty Crocker skills and it brings all of us so much joy to share these moments together. May the loving and purposeful energy in this “treasured” cookbook bless you and future generations; the previous owner may be smiling down on you.

author: @CrazyDiamondShiningOn
text:
I just happened upon your video and didn't know what you were looking for until you picked it up. It actually made me cry. My grandmother had hers. My mother had hers and I had the one that my mother gifted me. I lived with my mom and took care of my mom. After she died my younger brother wanted the house sold. Locks ended up being changed and a special lock box was put on the front and back door. 

My mother's 73 years worth of her life was in that house. My 52 years worth of my life was in that house as were my children's things, baby clothes, family mementos, my mother's christening gown from 1948. My baby books, my kids baby books, Christmas ornaments, so many things. I had my original clothes from the 80s in there. I had my little dolls from the end of the 60s in the beginning of the 70s in there. My life was in that house. When my grandma died her cookbook was given to me. I walked out of that house with nothing but the clothes on my back. Now I don't have my grandmother's, my mother's on my own cookbook. My mom wrote a special message in there for me. It was as good as losing everything to a fire. All because the baby of the family wanted to and I quote, "Benefit from mom's death too." (Yeah I know I thought the same thing too. That evil little POS) He was so used to having things handed to him. He never worked more than a year in his life. He conveniently had two car accidents so he could go on disability. He hooked up with someone that was my age, 10+ years older than him. She worked for the government so she supported his butt. 

I on the other hand worked hard as a single mom and pulled myself up from the bottom and raised three kids and took care of my mom and shared the house payment and all the bills with her. But because my mom didn't die with a will, whatever she had would be left to her three kids after her debts were paid off. I continued to pay the mortgage on that home all by myself for 6 months before he pulled his crap. 

I'm sorry to get this out here but maybe I just needed to get it out. It hurts the most when it's the people you love the most who betray you. Mentally I was not well for a long time and I'm still not myself, not just because of this. There's so much more to it and that's another part that really hurts. I've already told you how I pulled myself up but that wasn't all I pulled myself up from. I fought breast cancer and I struggled but I did it.  In addition to that they needed to remove my ovaries and my uterus because of cysts and fibroids and complications there. Without my ovaries, I didn't have my hormones. I couldn't have hormone therapy because that would put me at a higher risk of having my breast cancer come back. The medications I had to take for cancer had an effect on my bones and I have severe arthritis all over my body. The removal of my ovaries threw me into an instant depression and it actually took me out of work where I had a huge nervous breakdown. Talk about embarrassing. I was a strong, determined, responsible mother before that. I had my head held high. I was a good mom and I know it. That was a good daughter. I was faithful and loyal to my family and the Lord. 

You think you know what depression is, but unless you've faced it yourself with the chemical imbalances in your body, you have no clue, absolutely no clue what depression is. I was suffering depression and I didn't know what it was. I hid in my room for 6 months before I called my doctor crying because I couldn't get out of bed and I couldn't leave my room and I couldn't go to work and I kept calling into work and I didn't know what was wrong with me. That was when he explained it to me. 

I'm not saying any of this for any pity I don't want anybody feeling sorry for me. God gave me trials to go through and I went through them. God took care of me. Jesus Christ was with me every step of the way. These were all learning experiences. My mom said they were meant to make me stronger than I was. My mom was very wise, like many are.

Both of my brothers knew exactly what was going on every step of the way in my life and my mother's life. Yet they both managed to stay away from myself and my mother. One brother walked away from everything and didn't want anything to do with anyone after that. And then there was the greedy one. He didn't give two rats asses if I was homeless and had to go and sleep on my best friend's couch, my daughter moved in with her friend, then I had to move in with her and her friend before we could get me situated in an apartment. I was lucky I had to support any encouragement that I did. My friends and my three kids were going to let me fall like that. I got through it and I'm thankful I had them and the Lord with me.

I wanted to move back to my home state, but all of my family are now gone. All I have left in the world are my three children. So I have to stay here in the state they live in, because other than that, I have no one. 

I wrestled with my ghosts and there's a lot from the past that haunts me. I have had to learn to let go. In a way I pretend like none of that ever happened. I can't think about the things that I lost because it hurts. I don't have any of the things to pass down to my daughter. I can't give her the Hope chest that my mother gave me. My dad bought it for my mom when they got married way back in 1967. All the family recipes from generations that were written down. My grandmother came to the US when she was 2 years old. She traveled here on a ship with her mom and siblings from Hungary. I have no photographs of the family. I don't have any pictures of My grandparents for my father or my mother or when they were little. My children's baby pictures are all gone. All the research on the family tree that I did for 22 years written in multiple notebooks in addition to many many books I purchased that backed up all of my information for our genealogy. 

I have to let it go. I did for the most part but it's still there. Every so often it comes up and taps me on the shoulder like this book did. If anybody does take the time to read this I thank you. I'm sorry if I brought you down. There was only one thing that I lost that meant the most to me and I hope someday I see her again when Jesus takes me home. Oh what I wouldn't give just to hug my mom one more time.

I keep telling myself it's all just stuff. Yoko once said to John, "When you're dead you can't take nothing with you but your soul." He took her words and put them in a song. That line from that song has always stuck with me. Something I need to keep reminding myself. It was just stuff. Things. The important things are the things that we CAN take with us. Our love of our family and our love, faith and trust in our Lord Jesus Christ. I could be the last person standing on the Earth, but I would never be alone because I have Him.

If you did read all of this I thank you. Love your family. Love your children. Thank the Lord everyday for everything he has given you good or bad. It was for a reason. He needed you to learn something and he knew you could handle it just like I did. I still struggle. Clearly. But now that I've had my little mini breakdown and I was able to get some of this out, it's time to pick myself up and move on again. My mom always told me that God never gives us more than we can handle. 

There was another thing my mom used to always say to me. I had forgotten about it. But after she died I was standing in her room and for some reason a piece of paper just fell off of her bed and onto the floor in front of me. It was strange because there were no fans on and no windows open. I picked up that piece of paper. She had written down what she had told me so many times before and it fell on the floor in front of me. I felt like she was saying it again. So before I tell you what it was, I will say thank you. Praise God everyday for the wonderful gift of life and it's many lessons. Say a prayer for someone else. There are many people out there that are still struggling and need prayers. We can't all be everywhere at once. But no matter where we are we can talk to God at any time. 

My final gift from my mom: 

Don't worry about anything, instead pray about everything. Tell God your needs and don't forget to thank him for His answers. If you do this you will experience God's peace, which is far more wonderful than the human mind can understand. His peace will keep your thoughts and your hearts quiet and at rest as you trust in Christ Jesus.    Philippians 4:6

God bless you

author: @lanaserenelife
text:
Hello all :)

About to go to sleep for the first time in over 35 hours after this lol 😴 but just wanted to clarify a few things!:

I was out until 1:15 AM driving my mom and sister to the airport. They are gone this weekend. I discovered I was locked out once I got home. 

Had they been home, I would've just drove straight there. 

I do not have a key to their home. This is the second time they're ever both gone away at the same time, so we've never thought to give me an official key because one of them is always there when I visit.

Why didn't you:

Go to a hotel? 
- I could not have imagined this would take 9 hours. I thought it would be done by 4 AM latest, they also kept promising someone would call. By the time I realized they weren't calling, it wasn't financially worth it, hotels in my area are around $200/night. I'd rather skip one night of sleep honestly, especially if I'm getting there at 5 AM.

Sleep in your parking garage?
- My parking garage is freezing cold, last month there was a break in where things got stolen, I had no blankets, and most of all: there's no cell phone service in an underground parking garage! I also know myself and my sleep habits and it's unlikely I'd even be able to fall asleep.

Stay in your lobby?
We are all different. Some people could've stayed there all night and felt good with that, I stayed in my lobby for 2 hours and couldn't take it anymore. I can't stay stagnant that long. I love driving, I say it all the time, night drives don't scare me, and it's safer to be moving in my opinion. Please also keep in mind I worked 2 AM closing shifts at the movie theater for almost 5 years, and have solo road tripped many states alone at night! While I'm cautious, my town does not scare me.

Thank you to the lovely people who have been so nice and helpful! It was a difficult night, but I'm grateful for you. Also very excited to go to sleep right now. Goodnight everyone ❤️
video_title: locked out of my apartment and no where to go (all nighter vlog)

author: @TheodoraWohler
text:
This just happened to me, I just moved in a couple of weeks ago.  I am not from this area and have no family close by 
I went to the Police Station because I just didn’t know what to do.  They were great!  They called the 24 hour emergency number after I tried several times.  
Interestingly enough the property manager was there within the hour. The police said to the manager, this was a dangerous situation.  If emergency services needed in the building and there was no access, the management company could be held accountable.   
The officer was filing 
an investigation report and a detective from the department would be following up with the management company.  
I was hesitant about going to the police but they said I did the right thing.

text:
It’s completely unacceptable for a property manager to handle a lockout situation this way. If a tenant had the ability to call a locksmith immediately because management took far too long to respond, then the property manager should absolutely reimburse and compensate them for that expense.

This isn’t just an inconvenience — it’s a serious safety issue. As others have pointed out, what if someone left the stove on, had a pet trapped inside, or there was another emergency situation? Tenants deserve timely assistance and professional communication, especially in situations that directly impact their safety and access to their home.
Glad you’re safe and sound!!

text:
How to get rid of black mold on wood 
Distilled white vinegar and 3% hydrogen peroxide kill black mold instantly on contact by penetrating porous wood fibers. Both neutralize spores without releasing toxic fumes. Avoid bleach; it only strips surface color, and its high water content can drive mold deeper into the wood

text: Please don’t use Pantene! It makes your hair waxy and greasy. It may make your hair shiny at first but it puts a waxy layer on your hair that weighs it down and makes the strands of your hair stick together. It also contains sulfates and silicones which are very harsh ingredients. So as someone who wants to be a hairstylist it makes me sooo mad when people use Pantene. A good brand that is affordable is Cera Ve!

author: @SmooseySmellings
text:
I am a 47 yr old woman who came to follow you many moons ago when my little nephew asked me to follow his favourite youtuber called Pewdiepie that was trying to get 100 million followers. I have watched you since, finding your videos wholesome and uplifting to pass the time. 

I want to say to you that it fills my heart with joy that men and boys of all ages have access to such a wonderful male role model. It's so important and very rare. Thank you for filling that space. And for all young people regardless of gender, you and Marzia have consistently shown up and shared what a healthy relationship looks like. Which is priceless.

Thank you both, for being so epic.
video_title: Ending the vlogs.

author: @Alcatraz-a
text: It’s incredibly rare to find someone who is extremely wealthy yet remains so polite and genuinely sincere. I truly believe PewDiePie is one of those rare individuals. It was such an admirable decision for him to scale back his YouTube career to focus on being a dad, and the fact that he consistently shared that journey with us through his vlogs was amazing. I’ve loved watching them all this time, so I’m honestly a bit sad that we won't get to see them anymore. But looking at it from a family perspective, he absolutely made the right choice. Whether he continues to live in Japan or moves elsewhere, I will always root for him.
video_title: Ending the vlogs.

author: @analopez7659
text:
Your story resonated deeply with me. I studied abroad in Granada, Spain, and during my first weekend there, I was invited to a club through an agency very similar to Manuel’s. The club was full of foreign female students, all brought there by the same agency.

I only had one drink that night, but shortly after, I felt terribly sick and blacked out. Fortunately, I managed to escape. I was drugged. Months later, a friend I met during the semester — another foreign student who attended the same event — confessed that she had also been drugged that night and r*p*d. Unlike me, she wasn’t able to get away.

Watching this video made me realize that our experience was not an isolated case. These agencies prey on vulnerable foreign female students and take advantage of them. Tragically, this seems to be far more common than most people realize.

author: @captainlou9677
text:
"Beware of people who pounce..." it's upsetting to me that we live in a world where women are targeted. I don't have a daughter but I have a sister. 

About 30 years ago I was visiting Los Angeles from living out of state and I went out with my sister and her friends. I was 23 and she was 22 at the time. I was a firefighter and I had friends in law enforcement where I worked as a firefighter. One of these guys had accompanied me to this LA trip because he wanted to see LA. 

About an hour of hanging out at a bar in Pasadena, I noticed my sister was very intoxicated. My sister at the time was known for out drinking people and being in the bar for a short time didn't make sense with how drunk she was. My buddy asked how much she had to drink and it turned out she had only two drinks. The second drink was bought for her by some guy at the bar. I asked her what he looked like and my buddy and I went looking for the guy. My sister had several friends at the bar along with me, my brother, and a few of my friends. Whoever bought her that drink probably realized she was with a lot of people and he left. We never found him. But I really wish we had. 

It makes me mad just thinking about it.

author: @transdimensionalantics8526
text: My mom used to make something she called okra no-gooey. You just slice the okra, along with onions, bell pepper, and anything else you wanna mix it with. Then mix together eggs, a splash of milk, salt pepper, and garlic powder. Add some butter into a pan and then add the eggs into the pan once the butter melts and then sprinkle the bell pepper/okra mixture into the pan evenly. Let it cook (no mixing) till a good portion of the eggs are set up, then flip it, trying not to break it. Kinda like a savory pancake, and eat it with soy sauce. You can also eat it on top of buttery rice but the rice was only something I added after my mom died. It was my favorite dish when I was little and I was a super picky kid.

author: @gik7910
text:
1:47 YES! Surinamese Okra Soup is my favorite soup. It has family members travelling across the Netherlands just to have some of my soup 😂. In my soup I use: 

- beef broth
- beef shank
- salted beef

- okra
- taro leafs
- yellow onion
- garlic
- tomato
- madame jeanette or habanero peper

- pimento balls
- salt
- black peper

- a little bit of sunflower oil
- a big soup pot
- a big pot
- a baking pan
- a cutting bord
- a knife
- garlic crusher (optional)

1. Cook the beef shanks with the beef broth and piment in the big soup pot till the meat is softer (bone should detach from the meat easily, tendernism style). If there is any bone marrow left in the bone throw the marrow (without the bone) back in the pot. Cut the beef into smaller bits (not too small tho it still needs to be around bite size). The now empty beef bones can be discarded. I tend to remove any veins out of the beef but that is not really necessary. Throw the beef back in the pot and add enough water to be considered a soup.

2. While you start the beef shank cook the salted beef in a different pot till there is almost no water left. Throw the water out and rinse the beef out to get rid of the salt.

3. Slice the salted beef in bits. Dice the onion, mince the garlic and dice the tomato. 

4. Put some sun flower oil in a baking pan (its function is to not have the contents stick to the pan, not so much oil that it becomes a deep fry), bake the salted beef a little crispy. Add the onions and garlic. When the onions are shiny add the tomato’s. Cook it a bit and add black peper.

5. Slice the okras (remove the top and the bottom) and the taro leafs (remove the stems). Add them to the pan with the beef shanks.

6. Add the salted beef mixture to the soup.

7. Now either add more beef broth to salt or add some salt to taste. Add more black peper to taste.

TIPS
- Now with the habanero or madame jeanette peper, if you like it hot add it at the start of step one. And leave it in the soup until your aunty snatches it out of the soup and into her soup bowl. If you are not really a fan of the heat add it at step five and remove it after the soup is done.

- I have also made it vegan once. Then skip the meat and add vegetable broth instead. 

- You can control the okra slime by drying the okras with a paper towel prior to slicing it.

- In true Surinamese style: sleeping soup is the best soup as we say. So, the day after making the soup it is often at peak taste. Having your soup sleep does make the salted beef and okra a bit softer than optimal tho. A possibility is adding everything but these 2 items on day one and then the salted beef and okra the next morning. I never do this tho, it is a hassle, plus my family tends to want to clean out that soup pot on day one already. 😂 Just saying, if you want to go Masterchef mode it could probably be done this way the best. Top flavor, crispy salted beef and a little firmer okra than you would have on day 2 of the soup. Just know that if you do this you are being a little extra about it. 


I hope you try it and thank me later 😂.
video_title: 16 Crops You Should Plant In June ASAP

Dear Kevin,

I've been growing tromboncino squash this year, and it has quickly become my new favourite squash. When it’s young, it works perfectly as a zucchini, but it can also be used as a winter squash once mature. I’ve also found it to be more resistant to certain diseases compared to other varieties.

author: @MonetFaulkner
text: Hey Epic Gardening friends! Love your videos! Been watching you for years! I’m a Louisiana native and love my okra. I’m not a fan of frying especially in my house. My easiest and favorite way to use okra is to harvest when medium size, dice and layer on a pan and bake in oven for about 5 min or until slightly dry. Then I place in portion size bags in the freezer and take out when I want to add to gumbo, soups, stews etc. You can even fry it at that point if you want. Hope this helps and keep on growing. 😊

author: @Zenten040
text:
Lauren, I know you understand how parasocial relationships work, and I completely respect that you don’t owe anyone details about your personal life. But what frustrates me is the mixed messaging saying you want to handle things privately while also using very emotionally charged or clickbait style titles that naturally invite speculation and engagement.

People are obviously going to react and become invested when content is framed that way. It’s not that viewers are entitled to your personal business, but it can sometimes feel like the audience’s concern and curiosity are being encouraged when it benefits the channel, and then dismissed when people respond to it.
I hope you understand this and can somehow understand a few of our perspectives,not that you’re entitled to care,but if you do want to be “authentic and drama free”.

author: @Monitschka
text:
The wallpaper, the plants, and the table parade convey the feeling of being in one single, vast jungle. Wonderful.
Incidentally, I, too, grew up with the calls of peacocks. Their cries carry a very long way, after all. Located about a twenty-minute walk from my parents' home in Graz was Schloss Eggenberg, with its free-roaming peacocks. We could hear them all the way at our house. We loved going there—whether we had visitors or simply on a Sunday afternoon. We were overjoyed whenever we found a beautiful feather every now and then. My route to school took me right past it. Whenever I see and hear the Lalande peacocks, those wonderful memories come flooding back. 🦚🦚🦚🦚🦚🦚

author: @CallieMasters5000
text: I predict you'll have success with your "Nicki Positano IRL" lifestyle travel. Next you'll have to put out products besides your books, maybe some ceramics, tiles, pillow covers, 🍋 soaps or scents. Identify a Tuscany scent to go with the Positano lemon, and get local manufacturers to make them your brand. Martha Stewart started the same way, as a caterer and then into books, products and TV shows. The biggest warning for you though, is to always have multiple backup people to help you in case you suddenly get sick or are otherwise unavailable, including Carlo, Skye, other people from your videos. And I suggest you bring in your dad to do a guest lecture one night, showing off a highlights video of his career projected on a screen outside by the pool, followed by him doing a few stories and answering questions. Maybe like 2O-30 minutes of talking, so it's not too much for him. Also, I wouldn't put too much emphasis on wine on your tours, as many people don't drink alcohol much or at all these days, there are plenty of wine tours out there, and you don't want hungover guests on hour-long hikes! Many visitors to France, Italy and Scotland enjoy seeing a little of the local businesses but don't actually want to drink it. Also, maybe include two options for activities each day: the Indie option that involves hour long walks, and a Holly option that is easier for people that would otherwise be unable to attend your tours.

That looks like so much fun!  Loving the garden.  I wasn't sure how well it would grow, just based on watching other channels that are specific to gardening and how much remediation they have to do to their soil.  Yours has turned out so well!

Carlo knows how to fix or build almost anything, and you have amazing videography, editing, and storytelling abilities.  What a wonderful set of skills, in addition to being able to socialize easily.  It's the perfect setting for doing things like this.  That was a LOT of work to do to pull it all together, but I imagine it gets a lot easier as each group comes through.  

Can't wait to see the next video and hear how it went! 💜

I still can't wrap my mind around how many people was hoping the grief WOULD 💀 me!! It was my 1st time realizing they were jealous of our love & life and stuck around JUST to monitor!!! The abuse, theft & cruelty I endured after almost granted their wishes!! It's been bad bishes like you that helped me reclaim the parts of me I thought were gone forever!! You made me realize that NOBODY ever hates on a raggedy bih 😂... so clearly I'm not her...but... I AM HER & you are TOO!! 😏🥂 Thank you for everything & being everything!! Keep making em sick girl!! 🫶🏾💋

author: @ItsNikkiBaby44
text: Oh Tiff hun, no matter if there are others are going through the same thing or may even have it worse, that does not make your feelings any less valid. You are still allowed to hurt, grieve, feel overwhelmed, or react however you need to after hearing difficult news and going through what you’re going through. Pain and emotions are not a competition, and your struggles still matter just as much. Please don’t let anyone even yourself make you feel you’re not allowed to feel the way you are. It’s not self absorbed, it’s not selfish it just is what it is and it’s ok! Remember none of this is your fault please don’t blame yourself for putting your mom and Matt through this. They love you unconditionally and would not want you blaming yourself or feeling guilty especially when as you said it’s out of your control. I do understand, I truly do but please be gentle on yourself.  You’re in my thoughts and prayers Tiff! Sending you strength and healing. Hugs! 🫂🤍🙏🏻

author: @outoftheklosset
text:
Congratulations!!! We line our drawers too and even the top of our bedside tables. I'm disabled and I need water to take my meds and if I spill I know I'm not ruining anything. It's like a thin piece of liner cut to the measurements of the table. Works for us! :) I also use a liner on my dresser where I sometimes put my hair products on so if they leak I'm not ruining my vintage dresser. 

I would recommend getting a smart control for the garage door. This way you use your phone to open it with your password instead of a remote that someone could break into your vehicle and press. I love it! And we also keep a key in a padded lock outside the home. That way if there was an emergency a friend, family member or heaven forbid emergency services can access. I had to use that once when there was an emergency and I was so relieved our door didn't need breaking in.

So happy for you all!!!❤ Very exciting to get chickens! My SIL has chickens and one of them let's me pick her up and hold her. It's the cutest thing! I would recommend following BlackbirdCoop and Purely Chickens. Remember that they like to scratch so sand is very disappointing to them and they'll not be happy!😂

author: @jeffreygunn3530
text: Telluride is great (but expensive). Before driving down there in the summer, though, check the calendar. They have several music festivals there, and it'll be super crowded on those weekends. Also, don't try to drive into Telluride itself. Drive to the nearby "suburb" of Mountain Village, park in the garage, and take the free gondola that goes into Telluride. Telluride is a 19th century mining town, and there is very little parking for visitors.

author: @LivingOnCash
text: Man, those are good looking bikes. This video takes me back to the '70s when I was riding a pan chopper. We'd cruise about 70 tops but try to stay on back roads. Back then you could ride 50 to 55 comfortably and not hold up traffic because everyone drove around those speeds. It was a simpler time.
video_title: 350 Miles on Panheads! (Will We Make It?) - Vlog 251

author: @douglas-hughes
text: I very much enjoyed this inaugural episode of the Fraternal Order of the Traveling Panhead Restorers.  Some would be keenly interested in the 50's bikes and they are indeed cool but I think the best part of these videos is the camaraderie and the "I've got you" support everyone clearly shows each other.
video_title: 350 Miles on Panheads! (Will We Make It?) - Vlog 251

author: @trishsmith552
text: Katie, I am an old woman. In my day (the 60s) we had Home Economics classes in middle school and high school. The metal utensil you used to spread the frosting on the fruit pizza was called a spatula (not the things used to flip food in the pan). I think those were called turners. About the only thing the spatula was used for was to level off measuring cups. In cake making there is a version of that called an offset spatula, used for spreading frosting. That's what you really needed for your pizza. It would keep your knuckles out of the frosting. And I'm with you girl. I can't roll a crust out round to save my life. You should see my flour tortillas lol

author: @ramonahierholzer3163
text: Your dark chocolate appreciation reminded me of a fond childhood memory. I grew up in my grandma's dance studio, so to speak, that was about 30 minutes away from my home.  .. traveled there a few times a week from the time I was 3 years old. There was a "Mall", this was in the early 60's, near by. About once a month my parents would let me go to a candy store in the Mall , on the way home. I would save up my money, which might be a dollar or two, and ask to buy a chunk of dark chocolate for that amount. 🥰 And I would eat and savor that "chunk" of dark chocolate until my next chance to visit that store again. 😊 Thank you for bringing that precious memory back to me. 🤗❤️❤️❤️
video_title: Honoring Those Who Gave All: Memorial Day Cookout | Sharing Recipes

author: @sunspots7r7
text: My grandson's first and middle names  honor two different soldiers that were in Afghanistan  with my son on two different deployments that  were killed there (His Birthday is Wednesday). My son got a purple heart on his  first deployment and has 100% disability from the VA because  they can not remove all the shrapnel from him. A direct ancestor  has been in every war  except the Spanish American and WW! (My grandfather got a deferment tin WW1  because he was the engineer for a mill that was making stuff for the  war effort). My grandfather also contributed by a few times riding around with people from the war dept,   showing them where and how to identify micah deposits  in western NC and upstate SC. I was in the Air Force during  Vietnam, but spent a few years in England , Europe and the middle east. Memorial  Day is very real for me.

author: @AshleysHomestead
text: Now I'm not so worried about my peppers. Here in Humboldt County California its still in the forties at night. It's currently raining right now. My peppers desperately need to be transplanted. I'll just add some of my deep litter composted chicken manure and shavings to the holes and they'll love it. I add it to all my holes when planting my vegetable garden. It's still been to cold at night. And I just now have been getting my garden setup and ready for planting. I setup a new area for my garden this year. We built a big redwood fence to keep the deer out and I love it. I'm a few weeks behind because of getting the fence in and the weather this year. I'm also so envious watching you guys because you are always so far ahead of me. But you always give me good ideas. Happy gardening everyone 🥳🥳🥳

author: @smas3256
text:
I came across info I'll be using in Ct Valley.  
Give ea. plant a cup of cool water/rain water before sunset at ground level. 
This kicks in the plant's immune system and the plant  repair/rejuvenate overnight. 
We rest and repair during sleep time, right?    Thank you Jacques. Loving all your veggies. Flowers  attracting pollinators and predatory insect to eat the bad guys.

text: try spraying leaves with 1 part milk/4 parts water to control powdery mildew. but spray in early morn because sunshine helps with chemical reaction. repeat every 5-7 days.

author: @CosmicDuskWolf
text: The one thing and maybe Jacque can try this (since he loves tomatoes) that I have had great success with tomatoes is planting the tomatoes closer together and they have a tendency to hold each other up. Especially with cherry tomatoes. Though I have found if ignored tomatoes tend to take over what ever ground I plant them in, so I do what my grandpa does and use containers. From everything I heard from people, this seems a little close to grow so many different kinds of peppers together. I grew up in the bay area, and I rarely remember having a problem with powdery mildew. Of course my grandpa taught me how to prune when the leaves looked bad, so maybe I did and didn't know what it was that I was pruning.
video_title: 2 Hours of Gardening in 26 Minutes

author: @TonyTheTerrible
text:
theres a noticeable difference in overall plant production when you pick breaker or ripe tomatoes. i see this all the time with my "super san marzano" tomatoes which have a thick skin and can stay on the plant for WEEKS while ripe. the next fruits dont even begin to ripen until the ripe ones are picked

also on the yellow leaves affected by powdery mildew: some tomatoes are resistant to PM, like the super san marzano that i have. what resistant means is that they still manage to function even with yellowy infected leaves. the plant i mentioned above looks like its on its last leg but it continues to support its tomatoes.

author: @Распутин-и5х
text: I follow a lot of different gardening creators from all over the world on youtube and one thing I always find fascinating is how different the issues people have in the garden are. You are blessed with a sublime climate for growing most things, but you have issues I will never have to battle with. In my 20+ years of gardening I have never seen a tomato with mildew. Cucumbers, squash and melons on the other hand are basically dead on arrival if the weather aint right. In your mostly dry climate the slug pressure likely isnt too great. If I mulched everything like you do, Id have nothing left within 1 month because the slugs would be throwing a party. Also, because of the rain here in central europe you can count yourself lucky if your tomatoes out in the open dont have blight by august. We have to have em in greenhouses or at least build a roof structure of sorts and even then its no guarantee you get spared. The florida weave works here too but you have to space out your tomatoes way more and reduce leaf mass or its an open invitation to fungi and youll have nothing but brown slop by mid july. Whenever I see you and Kevin have great harvests in april and march(a time I cant even start to plant out most things yet) I always wish I had your climate here. But then I see all your issues I dont have to battle with and realize, I dont have it all that bad after all. Im in something like a zone 8 equivalent here. Continental climate.

author: @AlconburyBlues
text: I have several Anker power banks. Most of them over a year old, and none of them have died or caused power surges. But I would never buy one with pre-attached cables. That's a poor design. If the cable goes bad, it renders the entire power bank useless. It's much cheaper to buy a standard Anker power bank, and have on hand, several separate cables, and just keep them in the bag that comes with the power bank.

author: @cephalogim
text:
Had to lock one of these Anker batteries in our refrigerated trailer during a medium sized camping event because it kept beeping loudly and the trailer was the only somewhat sound proof "room" we had access to. The poor owner had tried to turn it off for hours and was driving everyone up the walls with it.
Got send back to Anker the next day via DHL (poor DHL people!) and that's where the story ended for me.

author: @vtxgenie1
text:
I figured that retractable and/or permanently attached cables would be a problem, so I bought one of their power banks without those features and it's been working well for years, plus still has 100w charging for both my laptop and phone. 

Your issue reminds me more of the zhiyun x100 portable video light... the battery on that apparently sent a reverse charge into anything I plugged it into, frying my motherboard, then 2 chargers before I realized it was the battery, not anything I was trying to charge it with. 

With all the mystery factories making these parts, I'm willing to bet it's the same circuitry issue, so not the charging, but reverse voltage out of the battery.

author: @bellsdawne
text: So I am in dental hygiene school and one of my teachers is a myofunctional therapist. The claim that expanding your palate improves health isn’t completely false; however, everything I’ve been taught has shown promise in GROWING palates. If you use a removal palate widener from the ages 18m-12 years, it helps widen your airway and reduce the risk of developing sleep disordered breathing as an adult. BUT these are new studies and in adults it’s not the same bc the bones in your palate are fused. Orthodontia has a reputation of being much more painful and extensive in adults because the bone is more dense. I don’t know if this matters at all but it’s weird to see how they turned an emerging preventative therapy into red-pill content😢

author: @JayeCole
text:
FYI / clarification - GCSEs are not like the SATs and do not get you entrance into a university.

We have a different school system and secondary schools here in the UK are for ages 11-16 and end in exams which give you a “qualification”. Typically pupils will want at least 7 but sometimes they will get as many as 9 GCSE qualifications. 

From 16-18 students attend Sixth Form College which ends with exams called A-levels. These are more specialised and most students will get 3 and sometimes 4 of these qualifications. These are used to apply for university.

So her kids are not only not getting the equivalent of a high school diploma, but they aren’t even getting a middle school-ish level of education.

Without GCSEs it will be nearly impossible to get employment anywhere.

author: @KimOpperman
text:
You can go back and look at your Walmart order online and figure out what brand soil that was that was contaminated. I would definitely report this because they really need to do a recall on the soil. 

Back a couple years ago right around 2020-2022 ish- they had a huge problem with potting soil that was contaminated with all kinds of items that should never have been in soil. 

There was a huge investigation. They had determined that they were finding composted, plastic, diapers, and all kinds of junk. 

I remember my garden doing horrible for a couple years after having used that soil. Everything was dying or getting viruses. 

That was also the same time period that random packets of seeds were arriving in online orders and being sent out to people from Amazon,  that were also contaminated. 

They were coming in from certain foreign countries in East Asia, and probably intended as biowarfare.

Ohio Department of agriculture was investigating all of that, and asked  to send samples and seeds that they have not ordered.    

It’s crazy scary that we have to worry about dirt and garden seeds,  whether or not someone has tampered  with them.  

It makes praying  over our food,  garden,  and family all the more important.

author: @YourOrganizedAuntie
text: Oh wow, let me just start by saying this: the way you titled this video made my whole spirit stand up like it was suddenly being interviewed for a promotion it didn’t apply for. Truly, chef’s kiss, because the moment I saw productive week at home, my inner voice whispered “Luise… take notes, girl, because your version of productivity last week was alphabetizing your spices and then taking a three hour break to emotionally recover.” And oh my goodness, prepping for your first store opening? HELLO? That sent me spiraling in the best way. I once tried to prepare for a big milestone by making a to do list, only to realize I had written “breathe” three times because apparently my brain thinks oxygen is a task. I literally said out loud “okay Luise, get it together,” and my inner voice replied “I would, but I’m tired.” And the cooking part? Whew. One time I tried to meal prep for the week and ended up burning rice so badly the pot looked like it had survived a small apocalypse. I stood there waving a spoon in the air like “why is adulthood so violent,” while my smoke alarm judged me from above. And workouts? Please. I once joined a home workout challenge and halfway through the warmup I was already negotiating with myself like “listen… we tried.” But your video reminded me that productivity isn’t about perfection, it’s about showing up, even if showing up means doing errands in mismatched socks and prepping for big dreams with a slightly chaotic ponytail. The way you balance home life, ambition, and that store opening glow? Wow. You make the hustle feel human, hopeful, and honestly… hilarious in the most comforting way. Keep going, because you’re building something beautiful and I’m cheering like a proud auntie with snacks.
video_title: productive week at home: cooking, workouts & prepping for our first store opening

author: @lynnboyett5540
text:
Why am I gardening?
When I was thirteen, I listened to my grandfather talk about the Depression. He was a prolific gardener with a large, bountiful garden every year. I realized the cycle of circumstances and that I would probably face a “depression” in my lifetime. I’ve prepared.
Also, I’m been financially wiped out, to zero, three times for different reasons each time. I have the experience of knowing that all of it can happen to me even with the best of plans.
Now, the prices in my retirement years are rising very fast for a fixed income. I’m glad I’ve invested time in learning gardening early because the learning curve would have put me behind otherwise.
Look, if I can learn pressure canning at 64, anyone can.

author: @judymadge7347
text: I recently moved house.  We have 3 veg patches that hadn't been used in years and were desperate for nutrients! I've turned the soil, fertilised and sown some veg even here in Melbourne!  I got a worm farm for Mother's Day and am setting up more composting areas.  I'm watching the sun and shade to determine what to plant where, but, in the meantime, I've planted some peas, garlic, potatoes, broccoli, onions, lettuce and some flowers.  I'm growing my own veg as I question the nutritional value of today's veg in the supermarkets and I also do not want to pay such ridiculous prices!  And finally, I find a deep sense of peace when I'm growing my own food!  Dirty fingernails = my happy place!
video_title: Food Prices Are About to Surge… Start Growing NOW

author: @CBEEBLE
text:
What happened to the planters/furniture (assuming paid for with communal funds) on the roof terrace? All tenants/residents had keys which strongly suggests that access was not accidental or unauthorised. Even if the lease does not expressly grant access, the terrace appears to have been treated as an established amenity of the building rather than a private or restricted area.
You should write collectively to the managing agent and ask them to confirm who authorised the change of locks, what happened to the planters/furniture and the basis relied upon for withdrawing access from residents and leaseholders without consultation. They should also be asked to confirm whether the withdrawal of access is connected in any way to any proposed roof, airspace or upward development, or any intention by the freeholder to preserve or assert development rights over the roof area.
Unfortunately, if the lease does not explicitly grant roof access and access has merely been permitted historically, the position may be less straightforward. However, the fact that residents were given keys and the terrace appears to have been used as a shared amenity means it is entirely reasonable to ask for a clear explanation before accepting that access can simply be withdrawn.

author: @lqstar
text: There are several movie theaters near me that have restaurants inside them. Each section has their own wait staff and each chair has a button to call for service or you can use the QR to order to your seat. The price of the movie and food aren’t any more expensive than other places. The food tastes good and often has themed food and drinks for the bigger movies. Some of the theaters have regular seating with tables on the sides and others have more private seating like pods with chairs and larger tables. The pod seating often has blankets as well.
